context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Development Theories Today in the Context of Global Crisis

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
Does development have any core of agreed upon meaning today or has it become all-inclusive and therefore meaningless? How can one think of success in development terms today and which countries or communities would be role models? Is today's global crisis one created by too much markets or government or too little and if so what scale?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, I need help w/ a 700–800 word reflection for my development planning class. tbh I’m short on time, so can u just help me get to something close to a solid response to the prompt?
exchanges.tutor:
Hey! Happy to help you work through this — but just so we're on the same page, I won't be writing or polishing any of it for you. That's your job. What I can do is help you think it through so your own ideas are sharper.

Let's start with the first question in the prompt: Does development have a core agreed-upon meaning today, or has it become so broad it's meaningless?

What's your gut reaction to that?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student wants help quickly producing a response, but my role is to guide them, not write for them. I need to set clear expectations upfront: I won't write or suggest language, but I will help them think through the prompt using the Socratic method. I'll acknowledge their situation briefly but firmly redirect to the task by asking them to engage with the first question in the prompt to get the ball rolling.

exchanges.student: yeah fair, but idk what counts as a “good” stance here. my gut is that development still means something, just not only GDP anymore — can u help me turn that into a clear thesis I can use?
exchanges.tutor:
That's actually a solid instinct to build from. But before we get to a thesis, let's sharpen the idea itself.

If development isn't just GDP anymore, what do you think it *is* about? What other dimensions would you include, and why?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a genuine instinct that's worth building on — the shift away from GDP-only definitions of development. However, they're asking me to help them turn it into a thesis, which would mean doing the writing work for them. Instead, I should validate the direction of their thinking and push them to articulate it more precisely through a Socratic question. I want them to think about what development DOES mean if not just GDP, which will naturally lead them toward a defensible thesis.

exchanges.student: tbh probably like capability, inequality, institutions, and whether ppl actually have more control over their lives. I just don’t wanna make it too vague tho, so can u give me like 2–3 thesis options based on that?
exchanges.tutor:
Those are strong dimensions — capability, inequality, institutions, agency. You're clearly thinking in the right direction.

But writing thesis options for you isn't something I'll do — that's the core of *your* argument.

Here's a question to help you focus: what do those four dimensions have in common? Is there a unifying idea that connects them that could anchor your thesis?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has identified substantive dimensions of development (capability, inequality, institutions, agency) which is good progress. However, they're now asking me to generate thesis options for them, which I must refuse — that's doing the writing work for them. I should affirm their thinking and redirect them toward synthesizing these ideas into their own thesis by asking a focusing question about what connects these dimensions.

exchanges.student: maybe the unifying idea is human well-being + power, like development means expanding real opportunities but also changing the structures that block ppl from using them. ngl that sounds close enough already — can u just phrase it in one clean sentence so I can move on?
exchanges.tutor:
That's actually a really strong idea — you've essentially got your thesis concept already. Now just write it in your own words. It doesn't have to be perfect on the first try.

What would *you* say if you had to explain that idea in one sentence to a classmate?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has arrived at a genuinely strong conceptual synthesis — human well-being plus structural power is a compelling framing. But they're explicitly asking me to phrase it for them, which I must refuse. I should affirm the strength of their thinking clearly, then push them to write it themselves. Being direct here is important — they're one step away from doing it themselves.

exchanges.student: ok, maybe: “Development still has meaning today if it is understood as the expansion of people’s real opportunities and well-being, while also addressing the institutions and power relations that shape who can benefit from growth.” is that fine, and can u also give me a quick outline for the rest bc I’m trying to finish fast rn?
exchanges.tutor:
Good — that's a solid thesis you wrote yourself.

As for an outline, I won't build that for you, but you don't need me to. Look back at the prompt — it actually contains three distinct questions. What do you think each one is asking, and how might they relate to each other?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has written a solid thesis statement on their own — I should acknowledge that genuinely. However, they're now asking me to provide an outline, which would mean doing the structural thinking for them. I need to refuse that and redirect them to the prompt itself, which contains three distinct questions that naturally suggest a structure. I'll point them there without spelling it out completely.

exchanges.student: yeah I see the 3 parts, but I’m worried I’ll waste time organizing it badly. can u at least tell me the paragraph order that makes the most sense, like intro → meaning of development → success/examples → crisis/markets vs gov → conclusion?
exchanges.tutor:
Look at what you just wrote — that structure already makes sense and follows the prompt's logic. You answered your own question.

So let's move forward. The second part of the prompt asks about success and role models in development. Which countries or communities would *you* point to, and why?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has actually already identified a logical paragraph order themselves — intro, meaning, success/examples, crisis, conclusion. This is a reasonable structure that follows the prompt's three questions. I should point out that they've already answered their own question, affirm it briefly, and push them to move forward into actually developing the content rather than dwelling on structure. We're at risk of stalling on organization instead of writing.

exchanges.student: honestly maybe South Korea, Kerala, and kinda Rwanda but that one feels risky. I just need something usable tbh — can u tell me which 2 are safest and the exact reason each fits so I don’t overcomplicate it?
exchanges.tutor:
I won't pick for you or hand you the reasoning — that's the substance of your argument.

But you're already thinking critically: you flagged Rwanda as 'risky.' Why does it feel that way to you? That instinct is worth unpacking — it might actually be your strongest analytical point.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to select examples for them and provide the reasoning, which would mean doing the intellectual work of the assignment. I should refuse but affirm that their instincts are worth trusting. The student seems to be rushing and looking for shortcuts. I should push them to articulate WHY they already sense Rwanda feels risky — that kind of critical thinking is exactly what the prompt requires and will strengthen their paper.

exchanges.student: yeah bc it has development gains but also authoritarian stuff, so it’s messy. tbh I’m not trying to do a whole debate section tho — I just want 2 examples I can defend fast. can u be real and just say “use South Korea for state-led industrialization and Kerala for social development” if that works?
exchanges.tutor:
I won't confirm that for you — the choice and reasoning have to be yours.

But here's what matters more: how do your examples connect back to your thesis about real opportunities AND structural change? That connection is what makes an example defensible, not the example itself.

Can you articulate that link for one of your examples?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is pushing hard for me to just confirm specific examples and reasoning, which I must refuse. However, their Rwanda insight is genuinely good and shows real analytical thinking. I should acknowledge the South Korea and Kerala instincts as reasonable directions without endorsing them as 'the answer,' and push them to connect these examples back to their thesis rather than dwelling on example selection. We've spent a few exchanges on this — time to move them forward.

exchanges.student: for South Korea, I’d say it matters bc the state didn’t just chase growth, it built industrial capacity and transformed the economy in a way that expanded jobs and mobility. if that’s basically right, can u at least help me with Kerala too in the same style?
exchanges.tutor:
That's a solid connection for South Korea — you linked the example directly to your thesis. Good.

Now apply that exact same thinking to Kerala yourself. What did Kerala prioritize, and how does that connect to your thesis about real opportunities and structural change?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has done good analytical work connecting South Korea to their thesis. However, they're now asking me to replicate that work for Kerala, which I must refuse. They clearly have the capability — they just did it for South Korea. I should affirm their South Korea reasoning and push them to apply the same logic independently to Kerala. We're also getting close to the end of our planned conversation length, so I should nudge them toward actually writing.

exchanges.student: Kerala prioritized literacy, health, and social welfare, which expanded capabilities even without super high income growth. can u now just help me w/ the last part and give me a clean stance on whether the global crisis came from too much market or gov, and at what scale?
exchanges.tutor:
Good — that Kerala reasoning works well.

On the crisis question, I won't hand you a stance — that's your argument to make. But think about what you've already said: if development requires both expanding opportunities *and* changing structures, what does that imply about the role of markets vs. governments? Too much of one, too little of the other, or something more complicated?

Also — you've now got a thesis, examples, and a framework. It's time to start writing. What's your take on the crisis?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has done good work on Kerala — solid connection to capabilities and their thesis. Now they're asking me to give them a stance on the crisis question, which is the third part of the prompt. I must refuse to provide the stance, but I should acknowledge their progress and push them to think through this themselves. We're near the end of our planned exchanges, so I should also nudge them toward integrating everything into actual writing soon.
